task_metadata.current_task: Use dot notation (.) as the key separator for nested levels
task_metadata.status: Algorithm design phase - deciding key format

nodes.description: Choose recursion as the core algorithm for flattening nested JSON structures
nodes.rationale: Recursion naturally handles arbitrary nesting depth without modification. It simplifies the logic: process each value, and if it's a dict, recurse into it. This is more maintainable than iterative approaches with explicit stack management.
nodes.task_state: Algorithm design phase - deciding traversal strategy

nodes.description: Deeply nested JSON structures may exceed Python's default recursion limit (typically 1000)
nodes.rationale: Python has a recursion limit to prevent stack overflow. Very deep nesting (>1000 levels) will raise RecursionError. This is a practical constraint for large documents.

nodes.description: Use dot notation (.) as the key separator for nested levels
nodes.rationale: Dot notation is the most common convention in JSON flattening libraries (pandas, jsonflattener). It's readable and unambiguous. Example: {'a': {'b': 1}} becomes {'a.b': 1}
nodes.task_state: Algorithm design phase - deciding key format

nodes.description: If original keys contain dots, flattened keys become ambiguous (cannot distinguish nesting levels from literal dots in key names)
nodes.rationale: Using dot as separator means 'a.b' cannot be distinguished from a literal key 'a.b' in the original structure. This creates a reversibility problem when re-inflating the structure.

edges.rationale: The recursive algorithm design is inherently limited by Python's recursion depth limit when handling deeply nested structures
Choosing dot notation as the separator directly creates ambiguity when original keys contain dots, limiting the algorithm's applicability
